summary:
The Iron Age in sub-Saharan Africa (c. 1000–500 BC in West Africa and the Great Lakes region, with variations by region) saw the independent development of iron smelting technology in multiple African locations — among the earliest in the world and predating many other regions' adoption of the technology. Evidence from the Nok culture of Nigeria, the Haya people of the Great Lakes region (modern Tanzania/Uganda), and the Bantu-speaking communities of Central Africa indicates that African iron-working developed with sophisticated techniques including high-temperature carbon steel production that in some cases exceeded contemporary Mediterranean technology.

African iron smelting was not simply imported from the Near East — archaeological evidence increasingly supports multiple independent inventions or very early adoption and adaptation. The spread of iron technology was closely linked to the [[event:1164|Bantu Migration Early Phase]] Bantu migrations: iron tools enabled forest clearance and agricultural expansion into previously inhospitable regions, and iron weapons gave migrating communities military advantages over earlier inhabitants using stone tools. Iron Age societies in Africa were often associated with significant social complexity, including trade networks, specialized craft production, and hierarchical political structures. The distinctive large-scale African furnace traditions — some producing temperatures exceeding 1400°C — represent sophisticated metallurgical knowledge accumulated over centuries.
